title: Emergency Guide: My Cat Swallowed an Ibuprofen Pill
meta_desc: If your cat swallowed ibuprofen, it is a life-threatening emergency. Learn the risks, why time is critical, and how a triage photo can save lives.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ibuprofen ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for cats that causes rapid, irreversible damage to the kidneys and stomach. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and take a photo of the medication packaging to help the medical team calculate the toxicity level.

What is ibuprofen toxicity and why is it dangerous for my cat?

Ibuprofen is a common human non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used to treat pain and fever. However, cats lack the specific liver enzymes needed to break down this medication. Even a single 200mg tablet can be fatal to an adult cat, causing rapid damage to the stomach lining and the kidneys.

Is ibuprofen ingestion in cats a medical emergency?

  • Yes, ingestion of ibuprofen is a high-level medical emergency.
  • You must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as irreversible organ damage can occur within hours of ingestion.

How does taking a photo of the medication packaging help the vet triage my cat?

  • If you still have the medication packaging, take a clear photo of the label and any remaining pills.
  • Sharing this photo with your vet allows them to identify the exact strength (milligrams) and the specific formulation of the drug.
  • This information is critical for calculating the toxicity level relative to your cat's weight.
  • A photo helps the medical team prepare the correct treatment before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ibuprofen is toxic to cats at approximately half the dosage that causes toxicosis in dogs due to their limited glucuronyl-conjugating capacity. In dogs, single ibuprofen ingestions of 100-125 mg/kg can cause vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, and anorexia; renal failure may follow dosages of 175-300 mg/kg, and CNS signs can be seen at dosages >400 mg/kg. Dosages >600 mg/kg are potentially lethal in dogs. Treatment of NSAID toxicosis consists of early decontamination via induced emesis and administration of activated charcoal with a cathartic, repeated every 6-8 hours. GI and kidney protection, along with supportive care, are also indicated; H1-receptor antagonists may be useful in treating GI ulcers.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3028)

faqs.question: What are the common signs of ibuprofen poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include vomiting (which may contain blood), abdominal pain, lethargy, loss of appetite, increased thirst, and in severe cases, tremors or seizures.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my cat vomit at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat using hydrogen peroxide or other home remedies unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian, as this can cause severe esophageal damage.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do for my cat?
faqs.answer: Treatment typically involves gastric lavage (pumping the stomach), administering activated charcoal to absorb toxins, and providing aggressive IV fluid therapy to protect the kidneys.
